cards.front: What does a goto statement do?
cards.back: It causes the program to jump to a statement identified by a label elsewhere in the same function.

cards.front: Can a goto statement jump to a label in a different function?
cards.back: No, goto can only jump to labels within the same function. It cannot jump between functions.

cards.text: A statement label ends with a {{c1::colon}} (:) and must be followed by a {{c2::statement}}.

cards.front: Why is using goto generally discouraged in modern C++?
cards.back: Goto creates spaghetti code that's hard to follow, understand, and debug. Modern control structures (loops, functions) are clearer and more maintainable.

cards.front: Are there any legitimate uses for goto in modern C++?
cards.back: Yes, very rare cases like breaking out of nested loops or resource cleanup in low-level code. But these are almost always better handled with functions or other control structures.

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.explanation: This creates a loop using goto. It prints x, increments it, and if x < 5, jumps back to the loop label. This is what while loops are for!

Goto creates "spaghetti code" because:
  • Execution flow jumps arbitrarily, making it hard to trace
  • Can jump backward and forward unpredictably
  • Makes reasoning about program state difficult
  • Bypasses normal scope and initialization rules
  • Modern alternatives (loops, functions) are clearer
Almost any goto can be replaced with better control structures.
examples:
// Bad: goto loop
label: doWork(); goto label;

cards.front: Can goto jump over variable initialization?
cards.back: The compiler will give an error if goto jumps over a variable initialization, as this could leave the variable in an indeterminate state.

cards.front: What is the one-sentence guideline for using goto in C++?
cards.back: Avoid goto statements entirely in modern C++ - use loops, functions, and other control structures instead.
